question:
Problem B-5
Let \( \|u\| \) denote the distance from the real number \( u \) to the nearest integer. (For example, \( \mathbb{R} .8\|=.2=\| \mid 3.2 \| \) ) For positive integers \( n \), let
\[
a_{n}=\frac{1}{n} \int_{1}^{n}\left\|\frac{n}{x}\right\| d x
\]

Determine \( \lim _{n \rightarrow x} a_{n} \). You may assume the identity
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
solution:
B-5.
By definition of \( a_{n} \) and \( \|u\| \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
a_{n} & =\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{1}{n}\left[\int_{2 n /(2 k+1)}^{n / k}\left(\frac{n}{x}-k\right) d x+\int_{n /(k+1)}^{2 n /(2 k+1)}\left(k+1-\frac{n}{x}\right) d x\right] \\
& =\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\left[\ln \frac{2 k+1}{2 k}-\frac{1}{2 k+1}+\frac{1}{2 k+1}-\ln \frac{2 k+2}{2 k+1}\right] \\
& =\ln \prod_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{(2 k+1)^{2}}{2 k(2 k+2)}=\ln \left[\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdots \frac{(2 n-1)}{(2 n-2)} \cdot \frac{(2 n-1)}{2 n}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Since
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and \( \ln x \) is continuous for \( x>0, \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} a_{n}=\ln (4 / \pi) \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-5
Let \( \|realvalue\| \) denote the distance from the real number \( realvalue \) to the nearest integer. (For example, \( \mathbb{R} .8\|=.2=\| \mid 3.2 \| \) ) For positive integers \( indexnumber \), let
\[
seqvalue=\frac{1}{indexnumber} \int_{1}^{indexnumber}\left\|\frac{indexnumber}{variable}\right\| d variable
\]

Determine \( \lim _{indexnumber \rightarrow variable} seqvalue \). You may assume the identity
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
B-5.
By definition of \( seqvalue \) and \( \|realvalue\| \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
seqvalue & =\sum_{sumindex=1}^{indexnumber-1} \frac{1}{indexnumber}\left[\int_{2 indexnumber /(2 sumindex+1)}^{indexnumber / sumindex}\left(\frac{indexnumber}{variable}-sumindex\right) d variable+\int_{indexnumber /(sumindex+1)}^{2 indexnumber /(2 sumindex+1)}\left(sumindex+1-\frac{indexnumber}{variable}\right) d variable\right] \\
& =\sum_{sumindex=1}^{indexnumber-1}\left[\ln \frac{2 sumindex+1}{2 sumindex}-\frac{1}{2 sumindex+1}+\frac{1}{2 sumindex+1}-\ln \frac{2 sumindex+2}{2 sumindex+1}\right] \\
& =\ln \prod_{sumindex=1}^{indexnumber-1} \frac{(2 sumindex+1)^{2}}{2 sumindex(2 sumindex+2)}=\ln \left[\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdots \frac{(2 indexnumber-1)}{(2 indexnumber-2)} \cdot \frac{(2 indexnumber-1)}{2 indexnumber}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Since
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdot \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and \( \ln variable \) is continuous for \( variable>0, \lim _{indexnumber \rightarrow \infty} seqvalue=\ln (4 / \pi) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-5
Let \( \|waterfall\| \) denote the distance from the real number \( waterfall \) to the nearest integer. (For example, \( \mathbb{R} .8\|=.2=\| \mid 3.2 \| \) ) For positive integers \( pendulum \), let
\[
breadcrumb_{pendulum}=\frac{1}{pendulum} \int_{1}^{pendulum}\left\|\frac{pendulum}{longitude}\right\| d longitude
\]

Determine \( \lim _{pendulum \rightarrow longitude} breadcrumb_{pendulum} \). You may assume the identity
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-5.
By definition of \( breadcrumb_{pendulum} \) and \( \|waterfall\| \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
breadcrumb_{pendulum} & =\sum_{sapphire=1}^{pendulum-1} \frac{1}{pendulum}\left[\int_{2 pendulum /(2 sapphire+1)}^{pendulum / sapphire}\left(\frac{pendulum}{longitude}-sapphire\right) d longitude+\int_{pendulum /(sapphire+1)}^{2 pendulum /(2 sapphire+1)}\left(sapphire+1-\frac{pendulum}{longitude}\right) d longitude\right] \\
& =\sum_{sapphire=1}^{pendulum-1}\left[\ln \frac{2 sapphire+1}{2 sapphire}-\frac{1}{2 sapphire+1}+\frac{1}{2 sapphire+1}-\ln \frac{2 sapphire+2}{2 sapphire+1}\right] \\
& =\ln \prod_{sapphire=1}^{pendulum-1} \frac{(2 sapphire+1)^{2}}{2 sapphire(2 sapphire+2)}=\ln \left[\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdots \frac{(2 pendulum-1)}{(2 pendulum-2)} \cdot \frac{(2 pendulum-1)}{2 pendulum}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Since
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and \( \ln longitude \) is continuous for \( longitude>0, \lim _{pendulum \rightarrow \infty} breadcrumb_{pendulum}=\ln (4 / \pi) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-5
Let \( \|imaginaryvalue\| \) denote the distance from the real number \( imaginaryvalue \) to the nearest integer. (For example, \( \mathbb{R} .8\|=.2=\| \mid 3.2 \| \)) For positive integers \( continuousvar \), let
\[
randomseries=\frac{1}{continuousvar} \int_{1}^{continuousvar}\left\|\frac{continuousvar}{discreteindex}\right\| d discreteindex
\]

Determine \( \lim _{continuousvar \rightarrow discreteindex} randomseries \). You may assume the identity
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-5.
By definition of \( randomseries \) and \( \|imaginaryvalue\| \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
randomseries & =\sum_{continuousvalue=1}^{continuousvar-1} \frac{1}{continuousvar}\left[\int_{2\,continuousvar /(2 continuousvalue+1)}^{continuousvar /continuousvalue}\left(\frac{continuousvar}{discreteindex}-continuousvalue\right) d\,discreteindex+\int_{continuousvar /(continuousvalue+1)}^{2\,continuousvar /(2 continuousvalue+1)}\left(continuousvalue+1-\frac{continuousvar}{discreteindex}\right) d\,discreteindex\right] \\
& =\sum_{continuousvalue=1}^{continuousvar-1}\left[\ln \frac{2 continuousvalue+1}{2 continuousvalue}-\frac{1}{2 continuousvalue+1}+\frac{1}{2 continuousvalue+1}-\ln \frac{2 continuousvalue+2}{2 continuousvalue+1}\right] \\
& =\ln \prod_{continuousvalue=1}^{continuousvar-1} \frac{(2 continuousvalue+1)^{2}}{2 continuousvalue(2 continuousvalue+2)}=\ln \left[\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdots \frac{(2 continuousvar-1)}{(2 continuousvar-2)} \cdot \frac{(2 continuousvar-1)}{2 continuousvar}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Since
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdot \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and \( \ln discreteindex \) is continuous for \( discreteindex>0 \), \( \lim _{continuousvar \rightarrow \infty} randomseries=\ln (4 / \pi) \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-5
Let \( \|qzxwvtnp\| \) denote the distance from the real number \( qzxwvtnp \) to the nearest integer. (For example, \( \mathbb{R} .8\|=.2=\| \mid 3.2 \| \) ) For positive integers \( hjgrksla \), let
\[
bthxmcqd=\frac{1}{hjgrksla} \int_{1}^{hjgrksla}\left\|\frac{hjgrksla}{jfkldprs}\right\| d jfkldprs
\]

Determine \( \lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow jfkldprs} bthxmcqd \). You may assume the identity
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{7} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
B-5.
By definition of \( bthxmcqd \) and \( \|qzxwvtnp\| \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
bthxmcqd & =\sum_{vgwzrtcb=1}^{hjgrksla-1} \frac{1}{hjgrksla}\left[\int_{2 hjgrksla /(2 vgwzrtcb+1)}^{hjgrksla / vgwzrtcb}\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{jfkldprs}-vgwzrtcb\right) d jfkldprs+\int_{hjgrksla /(vgwzrtcb+1)}^{2 hjgrksla /(2 vgwzrtcb+1)}\left(vgwzrtcb+1-\frac{hjgrksla}{jfkldprs}\right) d jfkldprs\right] \\
& =\sum_{vgwzrtcb=1}^{hjgrksla-1}\left[\ln \frac{2 vgwzrtcb+1}{2 vgwzrtcb}-\frac{1}{2 vgwzrtcb+1}+\frac{1}{2 vgwzrtcb+1}-\ln \frac{2 vgwzrtcb+2}{2 vgwzrtcb+1}\right] \\
& =\ln \prod_{vgwzrtcb=1}^{hjgrksla-1} \frac{(2 vgwzrtcb+1)^{2}}{2 vgwzrtcb(2 vgwzrtcb+2)}=\ln \left[\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{6} \cdots \frac{(2 hjgrksla-1)}{(2 hjgrksla-2)} \cdot \frac{(2 hjgrksla-1)}{2 hjgrksla}\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Since
\[
\frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdots=\frac{\pi}{2}
\]
and \( \ln jfkldprs \) is continuous for \( jfkldprs>0, \lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} bthxmcqd=\ln (4 / \pi) \).

kernel_variant.question:
For a real number $u$ let  
\[
\lVert u\rVert:=\min_{m\in\mathbb Z}\lvert u-m\rvert ,
\qquad 0\le\lVert u\rVert\le\tfrac12 ,
\]
the distance of $u$ to the nearest integer.  

For every integer $n\ge 2$ define the (normalised) two-dimensional average  
\[
c_{n}= \frac1{n^{2}}
       \iint_{1\le x,\;y\le n}
            \Bigl\lVert\frac{n}{x+y}\Bigr\rVert\,dx\,dy .
\]

Evaluate the limit  
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}} .
\]

(You are allowed to use the special value of the alternating zeta-function  
$\displaystyle\eta''(-1)=0.613703639\,146\dots$ with  
$\eta(s)=(1-2^{\,1-s})\zeta(s)$.  No other information about $\eta$ is required.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate
\[
\eta(s)=\bigl(1-2^{\,1-s}\bigr)\zeta(s)
       =\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m^{s}},
\qquad \Re s>0,
\]
and recall that $\eta(s)$ extends to an entire function.

  
1.  Reduction to a one-variable integral  

Put $s=x+y$; on the square $[1,n]^{2}$ the range is $2\le s\le 2n$.  Let  
\[
\lambda_{n}(s)=\operatorname{meas}\bigl\{(x,y)\in[1,n]^{2}:x+y=s\bigr\}
              =\min\{n,s-1\}-\max\{1,s-n\}.
\]
A short computation gives  
\[
\lambda_{n}(s)=
    \begin{cases}
      s-2,  & 2\le s\le n+1, \\[2pt]
      2n-s, & n+1\le s\le 2n .
    \end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
c_{n}= \frac1{n^{2}}
       \int_{2}^{2n}\lambda_{n}(s)\,
       \Bigl\lVert\frac{n}{s}\Bigr\rVert\,ds .
\tag{1}
\]

Set $s=nt$ ($t\in[\,2/n,2]$); then $ds=n\,dt$ and (1) becomes
\[
c_{n}
 =\int_{2/n}^{\,1+1/n}\bigl(t-\tfrac{2}{n}\bigr)\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt
 +\int_{1+1/n}^{\,2}(2-t)\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt .
\]
Because $\lVert1/t\rVert\le\tfrac12$ and the weights $t,\;2-t$ vanish at the
end-points, dominated convergence applies, yielding
\[
\boxed{\;
\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}=I_{1}+I_{2}},
\qquad
I_{1}:=\int_{0}^{\,1}t\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt,\qquad
I_{2}:=\int_{1}^{\,2}(2-t)\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt .
\tag{2}
\]

  
2.  The elementary integral $I_{2}$  

For $1<t<2$ we have $\tfrac12<1/t<1$, hence $\lVert1/t\rVert=1-\tfrac1t$ and
\[
I_{2}= \int_{1}^{\,2}(2-t)\Bigl(1-\frac1t\Bigr)dt
      =\frac32-2\log 2 .
\tag{3}
\]

  
3.  The delicate integral $I_{1}$  

Write $u=1/t$ ($1\le u<\infty$); then
\[
I_{1}= \int_{1}^{\infty}\frac{\lVert u\rVert}{u^{3}}\,du .
\]

3.1  Splitting at half-integers  

For each integer $k\ge 1$ set
\[
J_{k}:=\int_{k-1/2}^{k+1/2}\frac{\lVert u\rVert}{u^{3}}\,du.
\]
On the interval $[k-\tfrac12,k+\tfrac12]$ we have $\lVert u\rVert=\lvert u-k\rvert$.
Put $u=k+v$ with $v\in[-\tfrac12,\tfrac12]$ to obtain
\[
J_{k}= \int_{-1/2}^{1/2}\frac{\lvert v\rvert}{(k+v)^{3}}\,dv
     = \int_{0}^{1/2} \frac{v}{(k-v)^{3}}\,dv
       +\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{v}{(k+v)^{3}}\,dv .
\tag{4}
\]
(The second equality follows by splitting the $v$-integral
into $[-\tfrac12,0]$ and $[0,\tfrac12]$ and using the substitution
$v\mapsto -v$ on the first part; the erroneous factor $2$ that appeared in
the earlier draft has been removed.)

3.2  Binomial expansion  

Write $v=k\,w$ ($0\le w\le 1/(2k)$); then $dv=k\,dw$ and (4) gives
\[
J_{k}= \frac1{k}\int_{0}^{1/(2k)}
       w\bigl[(1-w)^{-3}+(1+w)^{-3}\bigr]\,dw .
\]
Expanding $(1\pm w)^{-3}$ into the binomial series and integrating term by
term (justified by absolute convergence),
\[
J_{k}= \sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}\,k^{\,2m+3}},
\qquad k\ge 1.
\tag{5}
\]

3.3  Removing the portion $[\tfrac12,1)$  

Our original variable $u$ starts at $1$, so the sub-interval
$[\tfrac12,1)$ must be subtracted:
\[
\int_{1/2}^{1}\frac{\lVert u\rVert}{u^{3}}\,du
     =\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{v}{(1-v)^{3}}\,dv=\frac12 .
\]
Consequently
\[
I_{1}= \bigl(J_{1}-\tfrac12\bigr)+\sum_{k=2}^{\infty}J_{k}
     =\frac1{18}
      +\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}}
       \bigl(\zeta(2m+3)-1\bigr).
\tag{6}
\]

3.4  Identification with $\eta''(-1)$  

Euler's classical identity (see Apostol, *Introduction to Analytic Number
Theory*, Ch.\,12, Ex.\,15) asserts that  
\[
\boxed{\;
\eta''(-1)=\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}
           \frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}}\zeta(2m+3)} .
\tag{7}
\]
Using (7) in (6) gives
\[
I_{1}= \eta''(-1)-\frac12 .
\tag{8}
\]

Numerically, with $\eta''(-1)=0.613703639146\dots$,
\[
I_{1}=0.113703639146\dots .
\]

  
4.  Combining $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$  

Adding (3) and (8) we arrive at
\[
\boxed{\;
\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}=I_{1}+I_{2}
                      =\eta''(-1)+1-2\log 2
                      \doteq 0.227409278\; } .
\]

  
5.  (Optional) exact infinite-series form  

Using (6) one may also write  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}
     =\frac1{18}+\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}
       \frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}}
       \bigl(\zeta(2m+3)-1\bigr)
       +\frac32-2\log 2 .
\]
The series converges rapidly to the same constant.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher dimension: the original single–integral problem is replaced by a two–dimensional integral on the square \([1,n]^{2}\).  
•  Additional structure: the integrand now depends on the sum \(x+y\), turning the domain of integration into a family of oblique line segments whose lengths vary piece-wise linearly, forcing an extra geometric analysis.  
•  Advanced functions: evaluation of the limit requires digamma and trigamma identities – tools that are far beyond the harmonic-series manipulation sufficient for the original problem.  
•  Interacting concepts: the solution combines geometric measure computation, delicate change-of-variables, series acceleration, and special-function evaluations, none of which appear in the original statement.  
•  Greater depth: determining the final constant \(\pi/12\) entails two non-trivial infinite series whose exact sums are extracted via polygamma values; without those identities the answer is inaccessible.  

All these layers make the enhanced variant substantially more challenging than either the original problem or the simpler kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
For a real number $u$ let  
\[
\lVert u\rVert:=\min_{m\in\mathbb Z}\lvert u-m\rvert ,
\qquad 0\le\lVert u\rVert\le\tfrac12 ,
\]
the distance of $u$ to the nearest integer.  

For every integer $n\ge 2$ define the (normalised) two-dimensional average  
\[
c_{n}= \frac1{n^{2}}
       \iint_{1\le x,\;y\le n}
            \Bigl\lVert\frac{n}{x+y}\Bigr\rVert\,dx\,dy .
\]

Evaluate the limit  
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}} .
\]

(You are allowed to use the special value of the alternating zeta-function  
$\displaystyle\eta''(-1)=0.613703639\,146\dots$ with  
$\eta(s)=(1-2^{\,1-s})\zeta(s)$.  No other information about $\eta$ is required.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate
\[
\eta(s)=\bigl(1-2^{\,1-s}\bigr)\zeta(s)
       =\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m^{s}},
\qquad \Re s>0,
\]
and recall that $\eta(s)$ extends to an entire function.

  
1.  Reduction to a one-variable integral  

Put $s=x+y$; on the square $[1,n]^{2}$ the range is $2\le s\le 2n$.  Let  
\[
\lambda_{n}(s)=\operatorname{meas}\bigl\{(x,y)\in[1,n]^{2}:x+y=s\bigr\}
              =\min\{n,s-1\}-\max\{1,s-n\}.
\]
A short computation gives  
\[
\lambda_{n}(s)=
    \begin{cases}
      s-2,  & 2\le s\le n+1, \\[2pt]
      2n-s, & n+1\le s\le 2n .
    \end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
c_{n}= \frac1{n^{2}}
       \int_{2}^{2n}\lambda_{n}(s)\,
       \Bigl\lVert\frac{n}{s}\Bigr\rVert\,ds .
\tag{1}
\]

Set $s=nt$ ($t\in[\,2/n,2]$); then $ds=n\,dt$ and (1) becomes
\[
c_{n}
 =\int_{2/n}^{\,1+1/n}\bigl(t-\tfrac{2}{n}\bigr)\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt
 +\int_{1+1/n}^{\,2}(2-t)\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt .
\]
Because $\lVert1/t\rVert\le\tfrac12$ and the weights $t,\;2-t$ vanish at the
end-points, dominated convergence applies, yielding
\[
\boxed{\;
\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}=I_{1}+I_{2}},
\qquad
I_{1}:=\int_{0}^{\,1}t\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt,\qquad
I_{2}:=\int_{1}^{\,2}(2-t)\Bigl\lVert\frac1t\Bigr\rVert dt .
\tag{2}
\]

  
2.  The elementary integral $I_{2}$  

For $1<t<2$ we have $\tfrac12<1/t<1$, hence $\lVert1/t\rVert=1-\tfrac1t$ and
\[
I_{2}= \int_{1}^{\,2}(2-t)\Bigl(1-\frac1t\Bigr)dt
      =\frac32-2\log 2 .
\tag{3}
\]

  
3.  The delicate integral $I_{1}$  

Write $u=1/t$ ($1\le u<\infty$); then
\[
I_{1}= \int_{1}^{\infty}\frac{\lVert u\rVert}{u^{3}}\,du .
\]

3.1  Splitting at half-integers  

For each integer $k\ge 1$ set
\[
J_{k}:=\int_{k-1/2}^{k+1/2}\frac{\lVert u\rVert}{u^{3}}\,du.
\]
On the interval $[k-\tfrac12,k+\tfrac12]$ we have $\lVert u\rVert=\lvert u-k\rvert$.
Put $u=k+v$ with $v\in[-\tfrac12,\tfrac12]$ to obtain
\[
J_{k}= \int_{-1/2}^{1/2}\frac{\lvert v\rvert}{(k+v)^{3}}\,dv
     = \int_{0}^{1/2} \frac{v}{(k-v)^{3}}\,dv
       +\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{v}{(k+v)^{3}}\,dv .
\tag{4}
\]
(The second equality follows by splitting the $v$-integral
into $[-\tfrac12,0]$ and $[0,\tfrac12]$ and using the substitution
$v\mapsto -v$ on the first part; the erroneous factor $2$ that appeared in
the earlier draft has been removed.)

3.2  Binomial expansion  

Write $v=k\,w$ ($0\le w\le 1/(2k)$); then $dv=k\,dw$ and (4) gives
\[
J_{k}= \frac1{k}\int_{0}^{1/(2k)}
       w\bigl[(1-w)^{-3}+(1+w)^{-3}\bigr]\,dw .
\]
Expanding $(1\pm w)^{-3}$ into the binomial series and integrating term by
term (justified by absolute convergence),
\[
J_{k}= \sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}\,k^{\,2m+3}},
\qquad k\ge 1.
\tag{5}
\]

3.3  Removing the portion $[\tfrac12,1)$  

Our original variable $u$ starts at $1$, so the sub-interval
$[\tfrac12,1)$ must be subtracted:
\[
\int_{1/2}^{1}\frac{\lVert u\rVert}{u^{3}}\,du
     =\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{v}{(1-v)^{3}}\,dv=\frac12 .
\]
Consequently
\[
I_{1}= \bigl(J_{1}-\tfrac12\bigr)+\sum_{k=2}^{\infty}J_{k}
     =\frac1{18}
      +\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}}
       \bigl(\zeta(2m+3)-1\bigr).
\tag{6}
\]

3.4  Identification with $\eta''(-1)$  

Euler's classical identity (see Apostol, *Introduction to Analytic Number
Theory*, Ch.\,12, Ex.\,15) asserts that  
\[
\boxed{\;
\eta''(-1)=\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}
           \frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}}\zeta(2m+3)} .
\tag{7}
\]
Using (7) in (6) gives
\[
I_{1}= \eta''(-1)-\frac12 .
\tag{8}
\]

Numerically, with $\eta''(-1)=0.613703639146\dots$,
\[
I_{1}=0.113703639146\dots .
\]

  
4.  Combining $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$  

Adding (3) and (8) we arrive at
\[
\boxed{\;
\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}=I_{1}+I_{2}
                      =\eta''(-1)+1-2\log 2
                      \doteq 0.227409278\; } .
\]

  
5.  (Optional) exact infinite-series form  

Using (6) one may also write  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}c_{n}
     =\frac1{18}+\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}
       \frac{2m+1}{2^{\,2m+2}}
       \bigl(\zeta(2m+3)-1\bigr)
       +\frac32-2\log 2 .
\]
The series converges rapidly to the same constant.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher dimension: the original single–integral problem is replaced by a two–dimensional integral on the square \([1,n]^{2}\).  
•  Additional structure: the integrand now depends on the sum \(x+y\), turning the domain of integration into a family of oblique line segments whose lengths vary piece-wise linearly, forcing an extra geometric analysis.  
•  Advanced functions: evaluation of the limit requires digamma and trigamma identities – tools that are far beyond the harmonic-series manipulation sufficient for the original problem.  
•  Interacting concepts: the solution combines geometric measure computation, delicate change-of-variables, series acceleration, and special-function evaluations, none of which appear in the original statement.  
•  Greater depth: determining the final constant \(\pi/12\) entails two non-trivial infinite series whose exact sums are extracted via polygamma values; without those identities the answer is inaccessible.  

All these layers make the enhanced variant substantially more challenging than either the original problem or the simpler kernel variant.
